articles.title: Behind the Scenes: The Role of Body Doubles in Modern Filmmaking
articles.summary: The article explores the often-overlooked contributions of body doubles in the film industry, focusing on the 2026 adaptation of Wuthering Heights directed by Emerald Fennell. While the movie sparked debate among audiences and critics, a particular scene featuring Heathcliff's scarred back became a point of fascination. Many assumed it was actor Jacob Elordi, but it could have been a body double. The BBC spoke with Bo Ponomari, a Ukrainian actor and body double who worked on the film. Despite being nine inches shorter than Elordi, Ponomari was cast due to his similar physique. He underwent an hour of makeup to apply prosthetic scars matching Elordi's, with director Fennell ensuring accuracy. On set, Ponomari assisted with testing camera angles, lighting, and provided alternative shots when Elordi was unavailable. He notes that final films often blend scenes from actors, stunt performers, and body doubles, making it unclear who appears on screen. The article sheds light on the collaborative nature of filmmaking, where body doubles play crucial but invisible roles in bringing directors' visions to life.

articles.title: Understanding the Spring Statement: Economic Forecasts and Government Decisions
articles.summary: The Spring Statement, delivered by Chancellor Rachel Reeves, provides an update on the UK's economic outlook, including forecasts for growth, inflation, unemployment, government spending, and tax income. These forecasts are produced by the independent Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR), which monitors government finances and releases reports twice a year. While not as major as the Budget, the Spring Statement influences future decisions on tax and spending adjustments. This year's statement will not include an official assessment of whether the government meets its fiscal rules, such as the 'headroom' measure—previously reported as £21.7 billion in reserve—which determines if spending cuts or tax rises are needed. Instead, it will cover policy changes announced since the last Budget, including eased inheritance tax for farms, business rate changes for pubs, and increased education spending for special educational needs and disabilities (SEND). The forecasts exclude potential impacts from recent oil price spikes due to strikes on Iran, but independent economists may still analyze the government's financial position. Speculation suggests borrowing and inflation could be revised down, while growth and job prospects might worsen.

articles.title: Debate Over Noise Increase at Boardmasters Festival
articles.summary: In Cornwall, the Boardmasters music festival has sparked controversy with plans to raise noise levels and extend performance hours. Organizers applied to increase decibel limits from 60 to 65 before 23:00 and from 50 to 55 between 23:00 and 01:00 from Thursday to Sunday, and to operate until 04:00. Festival director Rob Spring argues these changes are necessary to keep the event competitive and comparable to other festivals. However, local stakeholders, including hotel owners like Will Ashworth of Watergate Bay Hotel, councillors, and residents, have raised strong objections. They warn that higher noise levels could significantly increase disturbance, impacting businesses, campsites, and sleep quality. Newquay Town Council and Cornwall councillors have also expressed concerns, stating the proposals are likely to worsen noise nuisance. Festival organizers counter that the limits are an upper band, not a target, and they do not intend to cause unreasonable disturbance. A Cornwall Council licensing sub-committee is set to review the application, highlighting the tension between event promotion and community welfare.

articles.title: Drone Attacks Disrupt Amazon Web Services in Middle East Amid Conflict
articles.summary: Amazon Web Services (AWS), the cloud computing division of Amazon, reported that drone strikes damaged three of its facilities in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Bahrain, following US and Israeli military actions against Iran. The incidents occurred on Sunday, with AWS initially describing impacts as 'objects' hitting a UAE data center, leading to sparks and fire, while in Bahrain, proximity strikes caused power and connectivity issues. By Monday, AWS confirmed these were drone strikes, resulting in structural damage, disrupted power infrastructure, and additional water damage from fire suppression efforts. The company is working to restore services but warns that physical damage may prolong recovery times. AWS advised customers in the region to back up data and consider migrating workloads to other global facilities due to the unpredictable operating environment in the Middle East. This underscores the vulnerability of critical technology infrastructure, such as data centers, during geopolitical conflicts, as ongoing tensions between Iran and its adversaries pose risks to international business operations.

articles.title: Financial Struggles and Opportunities for Young Adults in the UK
articles.summary: This article explores the financial situations of young people in their 20s and 30s in the UK, focusing on two contrasting stories. Andrew Hall, a 24-year-old bartender in Guildford, faces significant challenges in the hospitality industry. He works irregular hours, often with last-minute changes, and struggles with rising rent costs. To manage, he uses payday apps to access wages early and saved £2,000 last year, but had to spend half due to reduced work hours. Feeling the stress is not worth the £15,000 annual pay, he has abandoned his career in hospitality and plans to attend university for better prospects. In contrast, Jack Wood, a 24-year-old technical operator in Salford, benefited from falling interest rates, which dropped from 5.25% to 3.75%, helping him and his girlfriend purchase their first home. He saved money by living at home with low rent from his £31,500 salary. The article connects these personal stories to broader economic updates, such as Chancellor Rachel Reeves' Spring Statement, highlighting how policy changes and industry conditions differently impact young workers' financial stability and future plans.

articles.title: Cillian Murphy and Cast Celebrate Peaky Blinders Film Premiere in Birmingham
articles.summary: Cillian Murphy joined a star-studded cast, including Rebecca Ferguson and Tim Roth, at the premiere of the Peaky Blinders film, The Immortal Man, in Birmingham. Hundreds of enthusiastic fans, many wearing flat caps and tweed in homage to the show, gathered at Symphony Hall to witness the event. The film extends Steven Knight's acclaimed TV series, with Murphy reprising his role as Tommy Shelby, a gang leader returning to Birmingham during World War Two. Knight emphasized that Birmingham, where much of the film was shot, is integral to the story, and Murphy echoed this sentiment, stating the city and Peaky Blinders are inseparable. The premiere was held in Birmingham to acknowledge the fans and the show's roots, as Knight shared that fan support has been crucial to the series' success from the start. The film will have a two-week cinema release before streaming on Netflix from March 20. Local celebrities, such as Duran Duran's John Taylor and athletes from Aston Villa, also attended, but the focus remained on the dedicated fans, like Claire White and Albert Laszlo who traveled from Derby.
